meta.description: MVP test slice #2. Covers the core political speech doctrinal line from Brandenburg (1969) through Counterman (2023), including the incitement test and true threats sub-doctrine. Pre-Brandenburg cases (Schenck, Abrams, Gitlow, Whitney, Dennis) are treated as historical background documented in notes rather than full case nodes.

areas.label: Core Political Speech
areas.description: Speech on matters of public concern — political, social, and governmental affairs. Occupies the highest position in the First Amendment hierarchy and receives the strongest protection. Government may not restrict core political speech based on content except in the narrowest circumstances: incitement to imminent lawless action, true threats, and a small number of other historically recognized categories.
areas.notes: Valid_from reflects Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969), which established the modern framework for core political speech protection. Earlier cases (Schenck 1919, Abrams 1919, Gitlow 1925, Whitney 1927, Dennis 1951) applied now-repudiated standards — clear and present danger, bad tendency, gravity-times-probability — all superseded by Brandenburg. The intellectual lineage runs from Holmes's Abrams dissent and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence through to the Brandenburg per curiam.

doctrines.label: Core Political Speech Doctrine
doctrines.description: Speech on matters of public concern receives the highest First Amendment protection. Content-based restrictions on core political speech are presumptively unconstitutional and subject to strict scrutiny. The government bears an extremely heavy burden to justify any restriction. Protection extends even to speech that is offensive, provocative, or distressing to its audience, provided it does not fall within a narrow category of historically unprotected speech.
elements: Speech addresses a matter of public concern
Government bears burden of demonstrating restriction falls within a recognized unprotected category
Unprotected categories are narrow and historically grounded: incitement, true threats, fighting words, obscenity
doctrines.notes: The categorical approach — identifying historically unprotected categories rather than balancing speech value against harm — is the governing framework. United States v. Stevens (2010) reaffirmed that the Court will not recognize new unprotected categories based on ad hoc cost-benefit analysis.

doctrines.label: Incitement Doctrine
doctrines.description: Speech that incites imminent lawless action is categorically excluded from First Amendment protection. The modern standard, established in Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969), requires that the speech be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action AND be likely to produce such action. Abstract advocacy of illegal conduct, even forceful advocacy, is protected. Only speech intended to and likely to cause immediate unlawful action falls outside the First Amendment.
elements: Speech must be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action
Speech must be likely to produce such imminent lawless action
Abstract advocacy of illegal conduct is protected
Both intent and likelihood of imminent harm are required
doctrines.notes: Pre-Brandenburg standards: Schenck (1919) clear-and-present-danger test; Gitlow (1925) bad-tendency test; Dennis (1951) gravity-times-probability reformulation. All superseded by Brandenburg. The intellectual foundation was Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927), both of which required imminence — a requirement Brandenburg elevated to majority doctrine 40+ years later. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: The doctrinal source of the Brandenburg test traces to non-majority opinions in earlier cases, not to any ESTABLISHES edge from a prior majority holding. The schema has no way to represent a dissent or concurrence as a doctrinal source.

doctrines.label: True Threats Doctrine
doctrines.description: True threats — statements where the speaker means to communicate a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against a particular person or group — are categorically excluded from First Amendment protection. The government need not prove the speaker intended to carry out the threat. Post-Counterman (2023), the government must prove at minimum that the defendant was subjectively aware that their communications could be perceived as threatening, rejecting a purely objective reasonable-person standard.
elements: Statement communicates a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence
Directed at a particular individual or group
Speaker must be subjectively aware the statement could be perceived as threatening (Counterman standard)
Government need not prove intent to carry out the threat
doctrines.notes: The subjective awareness standard from Counterman (2023) is a significant doctrinal development. Prior to Counterman, most circuits applied an objective reasonable-person standard — whether a reasonable person would interpret the statement as a threat — without requiring proof of the speaker's mental state. Counterman requires at minimum recklessness: the speaker consciously disregarded a substantial risk that their communications would be perceived as threatening. Justice Barrett's majority left open whether intent (purposeful) or knowledge suffices in addition to recklessness. Active circuit split developing on application. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: valid_from here reflects Counterman's refinement of the mens rea requirement, but the true threats category predates Counterman significantly (Virginia v. Black, 2003; R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul, 1992). The doctrine node has a single valid_from that cannot represent both the category's origin and the mens rea element's refinement.

doctrines.label: Public Concern Doctrine
doctrines.description: Whether speech addresses a matter of public concern is determined by its content, form, and context. Speech on matters of public concern receives full First Amendment protection. The public concern inquiry focuses on whether the speech can fairly be characterized as relating to any matter of political, social, or other concern to the community. Snyder v. Phelps (2011) confirmed that the public concern determination is a question of law for courts, not a jury question, and that context — including where speech occurs — does not strip facially public-concern speech of protection.
elements: Content: does the speech address political, social, or community affairs
Form: how was the speech expressed and where
Context: the circumstances surrounding the speech
Public concern determination is a question of law, not fact
doctrines.notes: Valid_from reflects Connick v. Myers (1983), which first articulated the public concern test in the public employee speech context. Snyder v. Phelps (2011) extended and clarified the doctrine in the private plaintiff tort context. The doctrine operates differently in different contexts: public employee speech (Connick/Garcetti line), private plaintiff IIED claims (Snyder), and content-neutral time/place/manner restrictions. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: This doctrine originates in a case (Connick) outside the current slice scope. The source_case_id pattern on DoctrinalTest assumes a single originating case. The public concern doctrine accumulated across multiple cases in multiple contexts, none of which is cleanly 'the' source.

doctrinal_tests.label: Brandenburg Incitement Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining when advocacy of illegal conduct loses First Amendment protection. Speech may be proscribed as incitement only if it meets both the intent prong (directed to producing imminent lawless action) and the likelihood prong (likely to produce such action). Both prongs must be satisfied. The test is conjunctive — meeting one prong is insufficient.

prongs.label: Directed to imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action. 'Directed to' requires intent — mere recklessness is insufficient. 'Imminent' excludes abstract advocacy of future illegality, however forceful. The speaker must be aiming at producing immediate unlawful conduct.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove both intent and imminence. Abstract advocacy, even of violent overthrow, is protected.
prongs.contest_note: The imminence requirement is contested at the margins. Courts have disagreed on how immediate 'imminent' must be — hours? days? Some circuits have found imminence satisfied where violence was threatened for the near future but not the immediate moment. Hess v. Indiana (1973) operationalized imminence to require that violence be directed at the present, not future, time.

prongs.label: Likely to produce imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be likely to produce the imminent lawless action. This is an objective probability assessment — is there a realistic likelihood that the speech will actually cause immediate unlawful conduct given the circumstances? Mere tendency to lead to violence, or likelihood of future harm, is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate realistic probability of imminent harm, not mere possibility or tendency.
prongs.contest_note: The likelihood prong intersects with context in ways courts have handled inconsistently. Online speech contexts have generated significant disagreement — when is violent speech online 'likely' to produce imminent violence, given the disconnect between online advocacy and real-world action? Post-January 6 litigation has brought this to the foreground in lower courts.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Brandenburg per curiam was brief and left significant interpretive questions open. The test's intellectual origin lies in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927) — both of which insisted on imminence as a constitutional requirement — but those were minority positions for 40+ years. SCHEMA ISSUE: source_case_id points to Brandenburg, but the doctrinal content traces to non-majority opinions in earlier cases. The schema cannot currently represent that the 'source' of a test's intellectual substance differs from the case that made it majority law.

doctrinal_tests.label: True Threats Test (Post-Counterman)
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional framework for evaluating whether a statement constitutes an unprotected true threat. Counterman v. Colorado (2023) held that the First Amendment requires proof of a subjective mental state — at minimum recklessness — before a statement can be punished as a true threat. A purely objective reasonable-person standard is constitutionally insufficient.

prongs.label: Objectively threatening statement
prongs.description: The statement must communicate a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against an identifiable individual or group. This is assessed from the perspective of a reasonable recipient in context.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the statement would be understood by a reasonable person as expressing serious violent intent.

prongs.label: Subjective mens rea — recklessness minimum
prongs.description: The speaker must have been subjectively aware of the threatening nature of the communications. At minimum, the speaker must have consciously disregarded a substantial and unjustifiable risk that their statements would be perceived as threatening. Higher mental states (knowledge, purpose) also suffice. Negligence — should have known — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove at minimum that the defendant recklessly disregarded the threatening nature of the communication. Objective reasonable-person standard alone is insufficient post-Counterman.
prongs.contest_note: Counterman's majority held that recklessness is the constitutional floor but left open whether higher mental states are required. Justice Barrett (majority) and Justice Thomas (dissent) disagreed sharply on whether the First Amendment requires any subjective mental state for true threats. Circuit courts are actively working out: (1) whether purpose or knowledge is required in some contexts; (2) how to instruct juries on recklessness; (3) whether Counterman applies retroactively to convictions under objective-standard statutes. Active circuit split expected to produce cert petition within 2-3 years.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Counterman is the Court's most significant true threats decision since Virginia v. Black (2003). The recklessness standard resolves a long-standing circuit split but creates new interpretive questions. Pre-Counterman, the majority of circuits applied purely objective standards; post-Counterman those standards are constitutionally deficient. State statutes with objective-only true threats standards are now vulnerable to facial or as-applied challenges.

cases.holding: The constitutional guarantees of free speech and free press do not permit a state to forbid advocacy of the use of force or law violation except where such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to produce such action. Ohio's Criminal Syndicalism Act, as applied to a Ku Klux Klan leader's speech at a rally, is unconstitutional.
cases.notes: Per curiam — no majority author. Black and Douglas concurred separately, arguing the clear-and-present-danger test should be abandoned entirely. The per curiam opinion effectively overruled Whitney v. California (1927) and rendered Dennis v. United States (1951) a dead letter without explicitly saying so for either. The test's intellectual lineage runs to Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). SCHEMA ISSUE: majority_author null creates a gap — the schema has no way to represent a per curiam opinion's authorial provenance or to note Black/Douglas concurrences as doctrinal sources.

cases.holding: A defendant's statement 'We'll take the fucking street later' — made as police were clearing antiwar demonstrators from a street — did not constitute incitement under Brandenburg because the words were not directed at any particular person or group, and the most that could be said was that the statement was advocacy of illegal action at some indefinite future time, which is not sufficient under Brandenburg.
cases.notes: Per curiam. Operationalized the imminence prong of Brandenburg — 'later' is not 'imminent.' Rehnquist, Burger, Douglas dissented. An important clarification of what Brandenburg's imminence requirement means in practice.

cases.holding: The First Amendment prohibits imposing civil liability on a civil rights organization for the violent acts of individual members based on speeches that included some violent rhetoric, where the speeches did not constitute incitement under Brandenburg. An organization cannot be held vicariously liable in tort for members' violence based on protected advocacy speech. The Brandenburg test applies in civil as well as criminal contexts.
cases.notes: Extended Brandenburg to civil liability contexts. Charles Evers's speeches to NAACP boycott supporters, which included statements that those who violated the boycott would be 'dealt with,' were protected political speech. Important for establishing that the Brandenburg framework applies symmetrically across legal contexts — not just criminal prosecution.

cases.holding: The First Amendment shields the Westboro Baptist Church from tort liability for intentional infliction of emotional distress arising from its picketing at a military funeral. The church's signs addressed matters of public concern — the nation's treatment of homosexuals and its wars — and were displayed on public land adjacent to a public street. The outrageousness of the message does not remove it from First Amendment protection when it addresses public concerns.
cases.notes: Alito dissented alone. The majority's public concern analysis is the doctrinally significant holding — Roberts's opinion establishes that the content/form/context framework for public concern applies in the private plaintiff tort context. The case did not address whether Westboro could be restricted under content-neutral time/place/manner rules.

cases.holding: The First Amendment requires proof of a subjective mental state — at minimum recklessness — before the government may punish a statement as a true threat. A purely objective standard asking only whether a reasonable person would find the statement threatening is constitutionally insufficient. Colorado's objective-standard stalking statute, as applied to Facebook messages sent to a woman by a man she did not know, required remand for application of the correct subjective standard.
cases.notes: Kagan majority joined by Roberts, Sotomayor, Kavanaugh, Jackson — unusual coalition. Thomas and Barrett dissented on different grounds: Thomas argued true threats have never required subjective intent historically; Barrett argued recklessness is too low a standard and purpose or knowledge should be required. The majority's recklessness floor is a compromise that neither wing found satisfying. Active circuit split on application is already developing.

edges.notes: Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: Brandenburg established this test as majority law, but the test's intellectual substance was articulated in dissents and concurrences in earlier cases — Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919), Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). source_case_id on DoctrinalTest, and ESTABLISHES edges generally, have no mechanism to represent this distinction between 'case that made this majority law' and 'non-majority opinions that originated the doctrine's content.'
Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

Direction: clarifies. Hess operationalized the imminence prong — 'later' is not imminent. This is a clarification, not a narrowing or expansion: it resolves ambiguity in prong 1 without changing the scope of the test.

Direction: expands. Claiborne extended the Brandenburg/incitement framework to civil liability contexts. The doctrine previously operated in criminal prosecution cases; Claiborne established it applies with equal force against civil tort claims, expanding the doctrine's scope of application.
MODIFIES is not present for the test node itself — Claiborne applied the Brandenburg test without changing its prongs. Applied the test in a civil context for the first time.

Direction: expands. Snyder extended the public concern doctrine from the public employee speech context (where Connick v. Myers originated it) to private plaintiff intentional infliction of emotional distress claims. The doctrine node's valid_from is 1983 (Connick), but Connick is outside this slice's scope. SCHEMA ISSUE B: This MODIFIES edge has Snyder as source, but the target doctrine originated in Connick (1983), which has no case node in this slice. The ESTABLISHES edge for this doctrine is missing — it would need to come from a Connick node that doesn't exist here. Cross-slice node dependency not yet formally supported.

Direction: clarifies. Counterman resolved the circuit split on the mens rea standard for true threats, settling that recklessness is the constitutional floor. The true threats category itself was established earlier (Virginia v. Black, 2003; R.A.V., 1992) — Counterman clarified the mental state element rather than creating the category. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The true_threats_doctrine node has valid_from: 2023 because that's when the current formulation became operative. But the category predates Counterman by decades. The ESTABLISHES edge for the category (from Virginia v. Black or R.A.V.) is missing — those cases are out of scope. Same cross-slice dependency problem as Issue B.
The post-Counterman true threats test is new — prior circuit standards varied and are now constitutionally deficient. Counterman is genuinely the source_case_id for this test formulation.

schema_issues_found.description: Dissents and concurrences as doctrinal sources. The Brandenburg test was established as majority law in 1969, but its intellectual substance originated in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). The schema has no mechanism to represent a non-majority opinion as a doctrinal source. ESTABLISHES edges run from Case nodes, and source_case_id on DoctrinalTest points to the case that made the test majority law — but this conflates 'legal establishment' with 'intellectual origin.' Proposed fix: add an optional intellectual_source field to DoctrinalTest (array of {case_id, opinion_type: majority|concurrence|dissent, author, note}). Alternatively, add an INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edge type between Case nodes with an opinion_type attribute.

schema_issues_found.location: majority_author: null on Brandenburg and Hess (Case nodes)
schema_issues_found.description: Per curiam opinions have no majority author, but the schema's majority_author field is a plain string with no formal null handling documented. More importantly, the schema has no way to record concurring opinions as doctrinal sources or to note that Black/Douglas's Brandenburg concurrences argued for a broader rule. The majority_author field needs: (1) explicit null semantics for per curiam; (2) an optional concurrences array with author and significance fields, parallel to the dissent pattern. This is related to Issue #7 but distinct — it affects the Case node schema, not just the edge layer.

Cross-slice node dependency gap. Several doctrines modeled in this slice originated in cases outside the slice's scope (public_concern_doctrine originates in Connick v. Myers, 1983; true_threats_doctrine as a category originates in R.A.V. v. St. Paul, 1992 and Virginia v. Black, 2003). The ESTABLISHES edges for these doctrines cannot be modeled without case nodes for Connick, R.A.V., and Virginia v. Black. The schema has no formal mechanism for a node to declare a dependency on a case node in another slice, or to represent a doctrine as 'partially established' pending a future slice addition. Proposed fix: add an established_by_pending field to Doctrine and DoctrinalTest nodes — an array of {case_id, slice_id, note} — documenting out-of-scope establishment cases. Alternatively, require stub Case nodes (id and citation only, status: stub) for any case referenced by an ESTABLISHES edge.

Single valid_from cannot represent layered doctrinal development. The true threats doctrine has two distinct temporal layers: (1) the category itself (unprotected since at least R.A.V., 1992); (2) the subjective mens rea requirement (Counterman, 2023). The node's valid_from can only capture one of these. This is a generalization of Commercial Speech Issue #5 (prong evolution) — here the problem is at the doctrine node level, not the prong level. A doctrine can have elements that became operative at different times. Proposed fix: add an elements_history array to Doctrine nodes, parallel to the prong valid_from/valid_until pattern in DoctrinalTest, allowing individual elements to carry their own validity windows.

APPLIES vs. MODIFIES tension on context-extension cases. Claiborne Hardware applied the Brandenburg test in a civil context for the first time. The schema rules say APPLIES is for cases that apply a doctrine 'without modifying it' — but extending a doctrine's applicability to a new legal context (civil vs. criminal) is a meaningful doctrinal development that APPLIES doesn't capture, while MODIFIES (which requires a direction attribute) seems too strong. 'Expands' on the incitement_doctrine MODIFIES edge captures the context extension, but the test node gets only APPLIES. Consider whether context-extension deserves its own direction value: 'extends' (applies doctrine in a new legal context without changing its prongs).

No RELATED_TO edges exist yet across either slice. The schema defines RELATED_TO as a cross-edge for non-hierarchical doctrinal relationships between Area nodes. Commercial speech and core political speech are sibling Area nodes under free_speech, but their relationship is more than just shared parentage — the public concern doctrine (core political speech) and the commercial speech doctrine share the same axis of analysis (what kind of speech is this?), and Sorrell (commercial speech) imports reasoning from the content-based restriction doctrine (core political speech). A RELATED_TO edge between commercial_speech and core_political_speech would be meaningful — but the schema gives no guidance on when RELATED_TO is appropriate vs. redundant vs. misleading. Needs editorial criteria.
